topic: Distributional synthetic control: sharp identification of the counterfactual outcome distribution for a treated unit under a quantile-rank-invariant interactive factor structure with finitely many donor units

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize when a finite donor panel point-identifies the treated unit's entire untreated counterfactual distribution under a quantile-rank-invariant interactive factor structure.

source_refs.claim: Distributional synthetic controls weight donor quantile functions to form a unique counterfactual quantile function, but the identification argument is not a finite-donor interactive quantile-factor boundary.
source_refs.author: Florian F. Gunsilius

source_refs.claim: Interactive fixed effects provide a mean-panel factor model and large-panel estimator, not a sharp finite-donor statement for rank-invariant outcome distributions.
source_refs.author: Jushan Bai

source_refs.claim: Distributional counterfactual analysis estimates a full conditional quantile function for one or few treated units in high-dimensional panels, but gives estimation bounds rather than a finite-donor sharp identification frontier.
source_refs.author: Ricardo Masini

source_refs.excerpt: sharp partial-identification theorem for the post-period treatment effect
routine support-function projection over a Chebyshev tube

open_problems.why_unsolved: The web literature splits the problem: distributional synthetic control supplies a quantile-function convex-synthesis object, while interactive fixed-effect and matrix-completion methods supply mean counterfactual machinery. The prior CausalSmith attempt supplied a scalar-outcome support interval under bounded factor misspecification, but reviewers judged it routine and explicitly noted that it did not cover a full counterfactual distribution or a quantile-rank-invariant factor law. The missing obstacle is the finite-donor rank/convex-hull condition separating unique distributional identification from set identification.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A necessary-and-sufficient identification formula for the counterfactual CDF or quantile function, plus a boundary condition stating when weaker finite-donor rank or hull assumptions yield only a sharp set.

open_problems.open_problem: Derive sharp counterfactual distribution bounds when distributional synthetic control has imperfect pre-treatment fit or the treated quantile path lies outside the donor distributional convex hull.

source_refs.claim: Imperfect pre-treatment fit is a recognized synthetic-control identification and bias problem, but the distributional sharp-set analogue is not developed there.
source_refs.author: Bruno Ferman and Cristine Pinto

source_refs.claim: Distributional SCM allows distributional effects and poor matching through pre-intervention quantile residuals, leaving open a finite-donor sharp identified-set characterization.
source_refs.author: Yi-Ting Chen

source_refs.claim: The paper studies asymptotic properties of DSC and notes the practical relevance of settings where treated quantiles cannot be perfectly fitted by weighted donor quantiles.
source_refs.author: Lu Zhang, Xiaomeng Zhang and Xinyu Zhang

source_refs.excerpt: Lift the scalar nonsharpness kernel from the two-point witness
State Conjecture 1 with an explicit constant

open_problems.why_unsolved: Imperfect fit is handled in mean synthetic-control bias and in distributional-SC estimation, but the sharp identified set for the whole untreated distribution is not pinned down by an RMSPE or integrated quantile-loss scalar. The prior proposal's reviewers asked for an open-set result with primitive constants rather than an assumed anisotropy condition, so the unresolved obstacle is to map signed pre-treatment distributional discrepancies into post-treatment distributional endpoints.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp bound or support-function theorem that takes the observed pre-period quantile residual process, donor quantile-factor restrictions, and a misspecification budget as inputs and returns attainable lower and upper CDF or quantile envelopes.

open_problems.open_problem: Determine whether convex, signed, and regularized donor weights that are pre-fit-equivalent remain identification-equivalent for the entire counterfactual distribution under finite-donor interactive factors.

source_refs.claim: Balancing, regression, difference-in-differences, and synthetic-control weights can be viewed in one framework, but this does not give distributional finite-donor sharpness equivalence.
source_refs.author: Nikolay Doudchenko and Guido W. Imbens

source_refs.claim: Matrix completion connects low-rank factor prediction and synthetic control for mean counterfactuals, suggesting an estimator lattice whose distributional analogue is not settled.
source_refs.author: Susan Athey, Mohsen Bayati, Nikolay Doudchenko, Guido Imbens and Khashayar Khosravi
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: The survey emphasizes ongoing comparisons between weighting, factor-model, and regularized panel estimators and notes that relative performance varies by context.
source_refs.author: Dmitry Arkhangelsky and Guido Imbens

source_refs.excerpt: RMSPE-equivalent and exact-factor-point-equivalent synthetic-control workflows need not be identification-equivalent
Replace K_sdid with the actual SDiD population first-order system

open_problems.why_unsolved: Mean-counterfactual literature has unified weight and low-rank estimators, and prior CausalSmith work tried to build a lattice among SCM, ASCM, SDiD, and matrix completion. Reviewers found those prior nodes too permissive or nonunique, and none of the published sources resolves whether two weight systems with the same pre-fit and same exact-factor mean prediction have the same distributional sharp set once quantile ranks and signed leverage enter.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A finite-donor rank/nullspace theorem or counterexample class specifying when pre-fit-equivalent donor weights induce identical versus strictly different sharp counterfactual CDF or quantile sets.

open_problems.open_problem: Separate marginal counterfactual distribution identification from full treatment-effect distribution identification under rank invariance, copula stability, or factor-rank restrictions in panel synthetic-control settings.

source_refs.claim: Changes-in-changes identifies a counterfactual distribution under a monotone structural model, but relies on strong rank structure rather than donor-factor synthesis.
source_refs.author: Susan Athey and Guido W. Imbens

source_refs.claim: Quantile treatment effects on the treated in DiD require distributional DiD plus dependence restrictions between outcome changes and initial untreated outcomes.
source_refs.author: Brantly Callaway and Tong Li

source_refs.claim: Distributional and quantile treatment effects in DiD are generally only partially identified, with panel data tightening but not eliminating the bounds.
source_refs.author: Yanqin Fan and Zhengfei Yu

source_refs.claim: Distributional treatment-effect parameters depend on the joint distribution of potential outcomes, which is not identified by standard assumptions alone.
source_refs.author: Patrick Kline

open_problems.why_unsolved: Distributional synthetic control naturally targets the marginal untreated counterfactual distribution for the treated unit, while quantile or rank treatment effects require coupling that untreated distribution with the observed treated outcome distribution. The cited DiD and CIC literature shows that the coupling is not automatic and often needs strong monotonicity or copula restrictions, but the finite-donor interactive-factor version of that taxonomy is not explicit.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A classification theorem stating which rank-invariance, copula-stability, or factor-rank assumptions identify only the marginal counterfactual distribution and which identify the full treatment-effect distribution or yield sharp copula bounds.

open_problems.open_problem: Identify valid donor-period cells for distributional synthetic control when group heterogeneity and latent quantile factors vary over time.

source_refs.claim: Group-heterogeneous distributional synthetic controls argue that valid controls may come from periods with comparable group heterogeneity rather than the same period.
source_refs.author: Songnian Chen and Junlong Feng

source_refs.claim: Generalized synthetic control unifies SCM and fixed effects through interactive fixed effects for average effects, showing why latent factors matter for donor construction.
source_refs.author: Yiqing Xu

source_refs.claim: The survey treats distributional effects, CIC, SC, and factor-model counterfactuals as connected but distinct strands whose assumptions are not yet unified.
source_refs.author: Dmitry Arkhangelsky and Guido Imbens

open_problems.why_unsolved: Standard distributional synthetic control often weights same-period donor distributions, while group-heterogeneous and interactive-factor settings suggest that the right comparison cells may be donor-period pairs with matching latent heterogeneity. The unresolved obstacle is that allowing cross-period donor cells can improve fit but risks extrapolating outside the rank-invariant counterfactual law unless an explicit latent-factor matching condition is imposed.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A donor-period selection rule with a sufficient-condition theorem for valid counterfactual CDF identification and a counterexample showing when cross-period distributional matching breaks causal interpretation.

literature_map:
Abadie and Gardeazabal (2003) and Abadie, Diamond, and Hainmueller (2010) anchor synthetic control for aggregate mean counterfactuals.
Bai (2009), Xu (2017), and Athey et al. (2021) connect synthetic control, interactive fixed effects, and low-rank matrix completion for mean outcomes.
Athey and Imbens (2006) identify counterfactual distributions through changes-in-changes under monotone rank structure.
Fan and Yu (2012), Callaway and Li (2019), and Kline (2020) show that distributional or treatment-effect distributions require extra dependence, copula, or rank restrictions and are often partially identified.
Chernozhukov, Fernandez-Val, and Melly (2013) give general counterfactual distribution inference, but not finite-donor distributional SC identification.
Chen (2020) and Gunsilius (2023) directly extend synthetic control to distributional objects via quantile residuals or weighted quantile functions.
Ferman and Pinto (2021) and Zhang, Zhang, and Zhang (2024) highlight imperfect fit and asymptotic behavior, leaving finite-donor sharp distributional bounds open.
Masini (2025) models the full conditional quantile function for one or few treated units in high-dimensional panels, but as estimation rather than a sharp finite-donor identification frontier.
Chen and Feng (2023) raise group heterogeneity and donor-period matching as a distributional-SC issue.
Recent robust and optimal-transport work suggests instability under support mismatch, multimodality, and donor correlation, but causal identification under such robustness devices remains unsettled.
The main tension is that mean SC/IFE has factor-model tools and distributional SC has quantile-function tools, but their finite-donor rank-invariant identification boundary is not yet characterized.
prior_proposal_map:
Direct eid_dist_synth_control_v1 contained only state/pipeline initialization, with no proposal or reviewer JSON to mine.
The highest-signal adjacent prior source was _bank/downgraded/pid_synth_control_factor_misspec_v1, which developed Chebyshev SC factor intervals and scalar-diagnostic nonsharpness but was downgraded for novelty.
Reviewer JSONs for pid_synth_control_factor_misspec_v1 supplied concrete strengthening paths: named SC workflow comparators, primitive open-set anisotropy conditions, and explicit constants/LP diagnostics.
The rejected angle pid_synth_control_factor_misspec_v1_proposal_angle1_rejected.tex supplied the convex-versus-signed-weight identification-equivalence gap.
Failed panel_sdid_mc_id_v1 supplied a related finite-donor SCM/ASCM/SDiD/matrix-completion equality-frontier gap.
Failed pid_qte_marginal_sensitivity_v1 supplied adjacent quantile-process compatibility material, but it was not emitted as a standalone gap because the current topic is synthetic control rather than marginal sensitivity.
The study-pipeline gaps were inspected as augmentation candidates but were LATE/Manski interval-regression topics and did not pass topical anchoring for this distributional synthetic-control run.
